title: South Carolina

jokes.text: Can you fuck off? / I have always tried to be there for you. / Well, how come you failed me in algebra, Mr. Hennick?
jokes.setup: Someone speaks to Selina in a sincere, emotional register — 'I have always tried to be there for you'
jokes.punchline: Selina treats it as a complaint from an old teacher about a failing grade, turning a moment of loyalty into petty grievance
jokes.explanation: The rapid-fire cut from 'Can you fuck off?' into what turns out to be Selina mishearing or misapplying a sentimental line is a sharp character beat — it shows her selective attention and pettiness simultaneously. Good economy, very Selina.

jokes.text: How would you like the chance to help lead a visibly repugnant underdog to historic victory? / You want me to join Jonah's campaign?
jokes.setup: Someone is being pitched a campaign job with grandiose framing
jokes.punchline: Amy immediately cuts through the spin: 'You want me to join Jonah's campaign?' — the descriptor 'visibly repugnant' landed as a sell
jokes.explanation: 'Visibly repugnant underdog' is a great bit of PR-speak that is technically honest — the joke is that it functions as a pitch. Amy's flat read of it as obvious strips away all pretense. Good economy and structure.

jokes.text: Men hate women, women hate themselves. The Geraldine Ferraro principle.
jokes.setup: 'Man up' continues to resonate in all four quadrants. It's universal.
jokes.punchline: Kent delivers the bleak sociological reason: misogyny from both sexes, branded as a historical principle
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The compression is extraordinary — it's a genuinely sharp political observation delivered in Kent's signature clinical data-analyst register. Naming it 'the Geraldine Ferraro principle' gives it the veneer of established political science. Could only be Kent. Quotable, dark, and surprisingly prescient. Earns the 8.5.

jokes.text: I'm feeling patriotic. / Full bald eagle.
jokes.setup: Selina asks for a waxing appointment in Charleston and adds 'I'm feeling patriotic'
jokes.punchline: Gary instantly decodes this as a full Brazilian / 'bald eagle' — the patriotic euphemism lands as both pun and character beat showing Gary's intimate knowledge of Selina
jokes.explanation: A tight, inventive euphemism joke. 'Bald eagle' as political-patriotic waxing terminology is genuinely original. Gary's instant, unfazed completion of the thought is the character joke inside the wordplay — it shows the absurd intimacy of their relationship. High quotability.

jokes.text: Dr. Jordan Thomas once said to me that I was the blackest white woman he ever met.
jokes.setup: Selina is making the case that Black voters love her and citing a civil rights leader
jokes.punchline: The anecdote she's so proud of is a hollow, mildly condescending compliment she has thoroughly misread as genuine praise
jokes.explanation: Selina's racial obliviousness is a sustained theme this episode and this is its first crystallization — she's citing a polite line she received as a badge of honor. The joke is entirely earned through character; her pride is the punchline.

jokes.text: Ma'am, you do know that this time you're running against an actual black person? / She's not even all-black.
jokes.setup: Ben gently points out the obvious flaw in Selina's racial self-confidence
jokes.punchline: Selina's first defense is to question the opponent's racial authenticity — an instant disqualifying rejoinder that reveals more about Selina than about Kemi
jokes.explanation: A crisp cringe beat. Ben's understatement makes Selina's non-sequitur comeback funnier. The exchange is tight and characterful.

jokes.text: Plus, I'm gonna be Lion-Kinging Little Richard all around till I tear my rotator cuff.
jokes.setup: Selina is explaining her outreach strategy to Black voters
jokes.punchline: She plans to physically hold up her Black grandchild like Simba from The Lion King until she injures herself — treating a baby as a campaign prop with gleeful commitment
jokes.explanation: An exceptional line. 'Lion-Kinging' as a verb is a genuinely fresh coinage; attaching it to a biracial infant as a vote-farming strategy is both appalling and hilarious. The physical comedy of 'till I tear my rotator cuff' adds a specific, self-aware commitment that elevates it further. Peak Selina: cynical, oblivious, and oddly earnest.

jokes.text: I have been working for you for 18 years without a promotion.
jokes.setup: Gary is making a timid pitch for a bigger role in the campaign
jokes.punchline: The understatement of Gary's situation — 18 years as a glorified body man with zero advancement — is the joke, delivered with his characteristic wounded sincerity
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat that reinforces the sad tragedy of Gary's existence. Not a standout line but it works in context.

jokes.text: OK, so give Gary the face-based thing. / It's 'faith-based.' / I don't care.
jokes.setup: Someone tells Selina their faith-based outreach program is a disaster
jokes.punchline: Selina mishears 'faith-based' as 'face-based,' assigns it to Gary anyway, and shuts down the correction with 'I don't care'
jokes.explanation: The rapid dismissal 'I don't care' after the correction is perfectly timed. Plants the faith-based video running gag.

jokes.text: No talking! / No talking! / I'm hearing moving!
jokes.setup: Selina wants quiet on the plane before landing; Dan tries to ask Ben something
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'no talking' decree gets Gary echo-enforcing it, then Selina escalates to banning even physical movement — 'I'm hearing moving!'
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'no talking' to 'no moving' is a tight comedic beat. Gary's sycophantic echo before Selina's own escalation is a nice structural element. 'I'm hearing moving!' is quotable.

jokes.text: OK, I'm gonna go hang myself from a sturdy pipe, and I'm not even gonna bother jacking off.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just gotten sixth place and is being told his endorsement can be sold 'for parts'
jokes.punchline: Jonah's suicidal ideation joke is immediately modified by the specificity that he won't even perform his usual self-comforting ritual — the escalating detail makes it funnier and more pathetic
jokes.explanation: A masterfully specific dark joke. The addition of 'and I'm not even gonna bother jacking off' recontextualizes a dark statement into a character study in a single clause — it's Jonah at his most pathetic and self-aware. The structure is impeccable: the setup establishes despair, the punchline reveals that masturbation is his usual consolation. Very Veep.

jokes.text: You have time for a drink beforehand? / Mmm.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just said he's going to hang himself
jokes.punchline: His advisor doesn't panic or help — he simply offers a drink first, and Jonah considers it
jokes.explanation: A perfect button on joke #11. The casual offer and Jonah's genuine consideration ('Mmm') lands because neither character treats the suicidal statement as anything but routine. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Don't math me, Lloyd. / Math is a plot invented by the Chinese to make smart Americans feel dumb.
jokes.setup: Lloyd correctly calculates that Jonah's rival did 33% better, having gotten 4% vs Jonah's 3%
jokes.punchline: Jonah rejects mathematics as a Chinese conspiracy — delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: A top-tier Jonah line. 'Don't math me' as a verb is a fresh coinage. The conspiracy theory is perfectly calibrated — specific enough to be funny, vague enough to be plausible for this character. Seeds the episode's running 'math is Islamic' escalation. Earns the 8.0.

jokes.text: Modern math was invented by Islamic scholars in the 7th century. / You were invented by Islamic scholars in the 7th century.
jokes.setup: Lloyd pedantically corrects Jonah's Chinese math conspiracy with a historical fact about Islamic scholars
jokes.punchline: Jonah turns the factual correction into a childish 'no you are' insult, which Lloyd's stepson then endorses with 'Burn. Sorry, Dad.'
jokes.explanation: The structure is tight: knowledge → deflection → childish burn. 'Burn. Sorry, Dad' from the stepson is a great button. Also seeds the episode's anti-math running gag.

jokes.text: Oh! Saint Theresa's gray-haired gunt, they're fucking again?!
jokes.setup: Jonah has just learned his mother and Lloyd are getting remarried
jokes.punchline: Jonah's profane shock at his mother's sex life, expressed through a bizarre religious oath invoking a saint's genitalia
jokes.explanation: A genuinely exceptional profane oath. 'Saint Theresa's gray-haired gunt' is inventive, specific, and absurd — combining religious invocation with anatomical disgust in a way that's startlingly original. The addition of 'they're fucking again' as the actual complaint makes it a complete comedic package. High quotability; classic Veep profanity craft.

jokes.text: I'll jam my fist up my dick hole and pull out a 40-piece set of Danish cutlery when you're president!
jokes.setup: Jonah threatens Jeff ('Uncle Judas') for backing another candidate, claiming he'll be president
jokes.punchline: Jeff's counter-threat is a grotesque anatomical impossibility used to express absolute disbelief in Jonah's prospects
jokes.explanation: Veep's profane hyperbole at its finest. The specific detail of 'Danish cutlery' elevates this from mere grossness into surreal comedy — the brand specificity makes it feel like a real object being threatened. Sets up a payoff callback later.

jokes.text: Selina Meyer is a legitimate candidate, not a human pool skimmer last used to de-spunk a Provincetown hot tub party!
jokes.setup: Jeff is defending Selina against Jonah's dismissiveness
jokes.punchline: The defense of Selina segues into a vivid, specific insult of Jonah as a used pool skimmer at a gay sex party
jokes.explanation: One of the best insults in the episode. 'Human pool skimmer' is already funny; 'last used to de-spunk a Provincetown hot tub party' is a masterclass in specificity comedy. The choice of Provincetown — a specific, culturally resonant location — makes the insult vivid and pointed. The craft here is in the precision: a lesser writer would've stopped at 'pool skimmer.'

jokes.text: Let me know where to send the gift. Spoiler alert: It's Danish cutlery.
jokes.setup: Jeff congratulates Jonah's sister on something; earlier Jeff threatened to pull Danish cutlery from his body
jokes.punchline: Jeff reveals the 'gift' is the cutlery — recontextualizing his earlier threat as a promised gift, completing the absurdist loop
jokes.explanation: A clean, well-structured callback that pays off the Danish cutlery gag. The 'Spoiler alert' framing is an efficient, modern delivery mechanism. Works because it recontextualizes a grotesque image as a normal social gesture.

jokes.text: Why are you Gary? / He had an early morning meeting. / You can't just replace Gary with another lesbian and think that I'm not gonna notice what-- / Mmm. Wow, this tea is the perfect temperature.
jokes.setup: Selina is startled by Marjorie bringing her tea instead of Gary
jokes.punchline: Selina begins a complaint about being given a replacement lesbian — but the tea is so perfectly made she trails off mid-sentence and forgets her grievance entirely
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered joke: (1) 'replace Gary with another lesbian' is a great casual reduction of people to categories, (2) the mid-sentence abandonment of the complaint because the tea is good is a perfect structural subversion. Selina's vanity and hedonism win out over her outrage. The structure is elegant.

jokes.text: Thank you, ma'am. I learned from an Afghani warlord. / We should put him on the payroll. / You killed him in a drone strike. / Oh, well.
jokes.setup: Marjorie explains her tea-making skills came from an Afghan warlord
jokes.punchline: Selina casually reveals she killed him in a drone strike and responds to this information with 'Oh, well' — complete moral indifference
jokes.explanation: A precision-crafted dark comedy beat. The escalation from tea temperature → warlord → drone strike → 'Oh, well' is a perfectly compressed three-act structure in four lines. 'Oh, well' as the moral summation of killing someone is the essence of Veep's political satire — casual executive indifference to lethal power. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: You've been taking fashion advice from a man who dresses like an overgrown ventriloquist dummy.
jokes.setup: Marjorie notes Selina hasn't paired the Bottega pumps with the Michael Kors top before
jokes.punchline: The implicit criticism: Selina's fashion has been guided by Gary, who dresses like a ventriloquist dummy
jokes.explanation: 'Overgrown ventriloquist dummy' is a specific, vivid insult for Gary that works because ventriloquist dummies have an inherently unsettling quality. Efficiently delivered.

jokes.text: The president hates bagels — shh-shh-shh — and she can't even be in the same room with donuts.
jokes.setup: Gary is briefing a new person and excitedly showing off the spread, which includes bagels from New York
jokes.punchline: Gary immediately panics and hushes himself after mentioning Selina's bagel hatred — treating a food preference like classified intelligence — and the donut detail escalates the absurdity
jokes.explanation: Gary's sycophantic terror around Selina's food preferences, hushing himself in a room she's not in, is a solid character beat. The shh-shh-shh self-censorship is a good visual/audio gag.

jokes.text: Mr. Walsh. This is your meeting. You're in charge. / Oh, I know. I know. I know. [pause] Of course. / Can I make anyone a plate?
jokes.setup: Gary is in a strategic meeting where he's been told he's in charge
jokes.punchline: After confirming multiple times that he knows he's in charge, Gary completely fails to lead and instead defaults to offering to make everyone a plate — reverting to his servant role under pressure
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed cringe beat. The triple 'I know' creates false confidence; the pause after 'Of course' is where the audience sees him processing — and then the collapse into 'Can I make anyone a plate?' is perfectly timed and totally character-true. Gary cannot escape his function as a manservant even when handed authority. High character integration.

jokes.text: What up, Mojo? When are you and I gonna have that pussy-eating contest?
jokes.setup: Selina enters, clearly in her new 'New Selina' bold mode
jokes.punchline: She opens with a sexual dare disguised as a greeting, to someone named Mojo — the aggressive vulgarity is completely unexpected from a presidential candidate greeting a constituent
jokes.explanation: Shock value with some character grounding — this is Selina performing her 'relatability' in the crudest possible way. The casualness sells it.

jokes.text: You have ten minutes. I started timing the moment you knocked.
jokes.setup: Selina is meeting with a rival senator to negotiate endorsements
jokes.punchline: Kemi has been timing the meeting from before Selina entered — asserting power with clinical efficiency and complete disdain
jokes.explanation: A neat power move delivered deadpan. The detail 'I started timing the moment you knocked' is the element that elevates it from generic to pointed.

jokes.text: I want vice president. / That's not negotiable. / Let's go. / No, I'm willing to negotiate. / What about Department of the Exterior? / Interior. / We're gonna negotiate against ourselves now, Amy?
jokes.setup: Amy is trying to negotiate Ryan's withdrawal from the race for Selina
jokes.punchline: Ryan torpedoes negotiations by demanding VP; Amy corrects his geography mistake ('Exterior/Interior'); Selina points out they've now undercut themselves
jokes.explanation: Multi-beat negotiation comedy. The 'Department of the Exterior' malapropism is a nice character moment for Ryan; Selina's exasperation about self-negotiating is the best line.

jokes.text: Remembering things and reporting them back to people is not my strong suit, but I could write it down. / Please don't write it down.
jokes.setup: Mike is asked to deliver a sensitive message to President Meyer
jokes.punchline: Mike openly admits his memory is terrible; his solution (writing it down) alarms the diplomat more than the original problem
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed Mike beat. His self-awareness about incompetence combined with his blithe offer of an even more dangerous solution is perfect character comedy. The diplomat's horrified 'Please don't write it down' is the ideal button.

jokes.text: It was in today's Line-By-Line. / You know I don't read what you give me.
jokes.setup: Selina is embarrassed about not knowing about the child shot by police in the community she's visiting
jokes.punchline: Marjorie points out it was in the briefing Selina demanded; Selina confirms she never reads her briefings — with zero shame
jokes.explanation: A functional character beat confirming Selina's executive negligence. Not a standout joke but solid contextual comedy.

jokes.text: My dad said that to a lot of white women. That was his move. Now you know.
jokes.setup: Selina has just proudly told Jordan Thomas Jr. that his father told her she was 'the blackest white woman he ever met'
jokes.punchline: The son reveals it was a scripted line his father used on many white women — deflating Selina's entire racial identity platform in one sentence
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and a masterclass in setup-and-payoff across scenes. The earlier joke was Selina's delusion; this is the demolition of it. 'That was his move. Now you know.' is delivered with perfect comic economy — almost kind, which makes it funnier. The structure across the episode earns the 9.0 on earned_vs_cheap.

jokes.text: Ask me what's on my mind. / Fine. What's on your mind, Congressman Ryan? / I'm glad you asked, because my mind's on my money... and my money's on my mind. / Laid back.
jokes.setup: In a serious endorsement negotiation with a senator, Ryan interrupts to do a Snoop Dogg reference
jokes.punchline: Ryan quotes 'Gin and Juice' as his negotiating position, then simply says 'Laid back' — his complete inability to read the room is the joke
jokes.explanation: Ryan's obliviousness played through pop culture non-sequitur. 'Laid back' as the self-satisfied button is a nicely timed beat. The joke functions because Ryan is entirely sincere.

jokes.text: I thought she'd be more familiar. I mean, did you at least watch Martin? 'Damn, Gina!'
jokes.setup: Jonah has just met a Black senator and is disappointed by the interaction
jokes.punchline: Jonah's idea of racial familiarity is quoting a 90s sitcom catchphrase — his clueless attempt at Black cultural connection is the cringe
jokes.explanation: Sharp cringe beat — Jonah's cultural reference is embarrassingly dated and inappropriate. Efficient delivery.

jokes.text: Could you give me HUD? Nobody cares about it. It's like the fat girl of Cabinet posts.
jokes.setup: Kemi is being asked what she'd want in exchange for cooperation
jokes.punchline: She dismisses HUD with a body-shaming metaphor for political capital — 'the fat girl of Cabinet posts'
jokes.explanation: A sharp, slightly transgressive metaphor. Works because it's delivered matter-of-factly by a Black female politician — the show refuses to let any character be straightforwardly virtuous.

jokes.text: What the tragic mulatto fuck? / Kemi looks like an albino. / And I'm so black, people are gonna start calling me articulate.
jokes.setup: Selina sees the anti-Kemi mail pieces she ordered and they've backfired — making Kemi look light-skinned and Selina look dark
jokes.punchline: Three escalating reactions: the opening oath, the observation about Kemi's skin, and the self-aware punchline about being 'called articulate' — a reclaimed racist micro-aggression
jokes.explanation: 'What the tragic mulatto fuck' is a startling, inventive oath. The escalating trio of reactions — ending with the sharp observation about being called 'articulate' (a classic racist micro-aggression against Black people, here applied to Selina) — shows real craft. The joke works on multiple levels simultaneously.

jokes.text: Man: Outreach. Reaching out. / No.
jokes.setup: Gary presents his faith-based outreach video with obvious excitement
jokes.punchline: The video is so bad that its opening — a man just saying 'Outreach. Reaching out.' — gets an immediate 'No' from Selina before it can continue
jokes.explanation: A precise reaction beat — the video's opening line is so aggressively bland ('Outreach. Reaching out.') that the single-word dismissal is all that's needed. Economy score is high because the joke is entirely in the execution.

jokes.text: Well, my polling shows their main wants are jobs, education, and an adequate safety net— / Okay, not gonna speak to that. / I'm not finished, ma'am. / '...to be denied to African Americans.'
jokes.setup: Selina asks what appeals to non-college-educated white voters; Kent begins with a seemingly reasonable answer
jokes.punchline: The sentence isn't complete — the full polling result is that they want those things denied to Black Americans. The pause and 'I'm not finished' makes the audience think a correction is coming, but it's a darker reveal
jokes.explanation: One of the most structurally accomplished jokes in the episode. The misdirection is perfect: the first half of the sentence sounds like legitimate policy; 'I'm not finished, ma'am' makes you think the good version is coming; the punchline is devastating. The comedy comes from the political truth embedded in it — dark, earned, and brilliantly constructed.

jokes.text: Like a dog whistle. / Yes, exactly! / Come on, that's like a dog whisper. / You could 'reject' an endorsement from a pro-Confederacy group. / That's like a dog exploding space shuttle. / I need something loud, not too loud like-- / Dog chainsaw? / No, that's too droney. / A dog snowmobile. / Why would I know what that even sounds like? / A dog leaf blower? / Yeah, I like that.
jokes.setup: Selina needs a political signal that will appeal to racists without being obviously racist — someone suggests a 'dog whistle'
jokes.punchline: The metaphor spawns an escalating game of inventing new dog-themed political signaling devices: dog whisper, dog exploding space shuttle, dog chainsaw, dog snowmobile, dog leaf blower
jokes.explanation: A sustained escalation joke that earns every step. The political metaphor of the 'dog whistle' is extended into absurdity through increasingly loud/specific instruments, each calibrated differently. 'Dog exploding space shuttle' is the peak absurdist moment; 'that's too droney' for chainsaw is the best internal logic gag. The team's collective wordplay feels genuinely improvised within structure.

jokes.text: Okay, guys, what you need to do is get me in front of huge white audiences. Huge. Huge. Something that makes a NASCAR race look like a Jay-Z concert, you know? / Actually, ma'am, Jay-Z concerts are almost all white people. / Okay, well, right? I know.
jokes.setup: Selina wants to reach the whitest possible voter demographic
jokes.punchline: Her reference point for 'all-white audience' is factually wrong — Jay-Z concerts are predominantly white — and she tries to save face with 'I know'
jokes.explanation: A topical observational joke layered with Selina's face-saving 'I know.' The observation is true and unexpected enough to land.

jokes.text: Okay, guys, what you need to do is get me in front of huge white audiences. Huge. Something that makes a NASCAR race look like a Jay-Z concert.
jokes.setup: Selina is strategizing about reaching white voters.
jokes.punchline: Her request for audiences 'whiter than a NASCAR race' — an explicit racial preference for her campaign events, stated without irony.
jokes.explanation: Competent political satire — the NASCAR/Jay-Z contrast is a serviceable cultural shorthand for racial demographic coding. The follow-on correction ('Jay-Z concerts are almost all white people') is a sharp reality check that makes Selina's framework look even more dated.

jokes.text: Where are you registered? Child Protective Services? / No, Gymboree.
jokes.setup: Mike announces he and Wendy are adopting another Chinese baby
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'registered at CPS' joke implies Mike is an unfit parent; Mike's guileless 'Gymboree' response completes it perfectly
jokes.explanation: Tight two-line joke. CPS as a baby registry is sharp; Mike's earnest 'Gymboree' makes it land harder because he completely missed the insult.

jokes.text: I love that they make clothes in your size now, Mike.
jokes.setup: Mike mentions Gymboree
jokes.punchline: Selina twists it into a body-shaming joke — implying Gymboree's toddler sizing now fits Mike
jokes.explanation: A functional but basic body-shaming gag. Works in context as a Selina cruelty beat but doesn't have the specificity of her best insults.

jokes.text: A misfortune cookie.
jokes.setup: Liu sent a sensitive diplomatic message through Mike — an informal, risky channel for state communications
jokes.punchline: Selina coins the term 'misfortune cookie' — a fortune cookie that brings bad news, delivered through a Chinese-American conduit
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. 'Misfortune cookie' is an elegantly compressed joke — it works as wordplay, as political commentary (Mike as an unwitting diplomatic channel), and as a Selina character beat (her wit at its sharpest). Three words. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Who cares about an island that doesn't have a Four Seasons on it?
jokes.setup: The team is discussing the geopolitical importance of the Diaoyu Islands
jokes.punchline: Selina reduces international sovereignty dispute to luxury hotel infrastructure
jokes.explanation: A crisp Selina character line. The Four Seasons standard as the unit of geopolitical worth is a perfectly specific and perfectly wrong metric. High character integration — this could only be Selina.

jokes.text: 'Ish.' / 'Esque.'
jokes.setup: Someone says 'we are still a nation of laws'
jokes.punchline: Ben and Selina simultaneously append legal qualifiers — 'ish' and 'esque' — to 'laws,' reducing the rule of law to a rough approximation
jokes.explanation: A masterfully economic joke. Two words capture an entire political worldview. The simultaneous delivery of 'ish' and 'esque' means both characters have this cynicism so deeply ingrained they don't even need to discuss it — they just instinctively qualify 'laws.' Perfect Veep political satire. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: We are not talking about letting the Chinese influence our presidential election... And, since it is not being discussed, then I do not have to mention that you cannot trust the Chinese. And they will not sell you out in a Beijing minute. / Wait, they will or they won't? / I don't know.
jokes.setup: Ben is explaining what they're 'not' discussing regarding Chinese election interference
jokes.punchline: Ben's double negative — 'cannot trust' / 'will not sell you out' — becomes genuinely confusing, and his honest 'I don't know' is the perfect button
jokes.explanation: A structurally clever bit where the very act of 'not discussing' the topic becomes its own confusion. Ben's befuddlement at his own syntax and 'I don't know' is a great self-undermining button.

jokes.text: Can't we 'not' not do the thing that we're not talking about?
jokes.setup: The team is tangled in double-negative deniability about Chinese election interference
jokes.punchline: Selina's attempt to clarify produces a quadruple negative that's grammatically and diplomatically meaningless — while meaning everything
jokes.explanation: A compact escalation on the 'plausible deniability' joke. The linguistic tangle is funny precisely because it communicates something while ostensibly communicating nothing.

jokes.text: Do you think Captain Lady Kangaroo heard any of that? / I don't think so. / No? Okay, good. / Fire her anyway just to be sure. / Consider her green-jeaned, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Selina and Ben wonder if Marjorie overheard their seditious conversation
jokes.punchline: They decide to fire her preemptively regardless; Marjorie's 'Consider her green-jeaned' is a deadpan confirmation that she was listening and is now accepting her own firing
jokes.explanation: 'Captain Lady Kangaroo' is a great insult coinage for Marjorie. The real joke is Marjorie's stoic 'Consider her green-jeaned' — accepting her own firing with robot efficiency while revealing she heard everything. Her deadpan is the punchline.

jokes.text: When my uncle stole me, I don't remember where he took me, but I do have this recurring dream where I almost find out.
jokes.setup: Catherine and Marjorie are arguing about taking Little Richard to church
jokes.punchline: Richard non-sequiturs into a deeply dark childhood trauma delivered with complete placidity — he was stolen by his uncle and has a recurring nightmare about almost uncovering what happened
jokes.explanation: The best Richard joke in the episode and one of the best in the series. The structure is perfect: the setup sounds like a comparable anecdote about being taken somewhere by a relative; the punchline reveals kidnapping AND a recurring nightmare about the unknown horror. Richard's complete lack of affect is essential — he delivers potential childhood trauma with the emotional register of describing weather. 'I almost find out' is the killer phrase: it implies the dream's mercy is that he never fully remembers.

jokes.text: Oh, it's a crop duster. They usually fly pretty low. / Not that low.
jokes.setup: Someone asks Richard about a noise; he calmly explains it's a crop duster
jokes.punchline: 'Not that low' — the plane is about to crash. Richard's calm assessment is immediately contradicted by reality
jokes.explanation: A crisp Buster Keaton-esque setup: the expert gives false reassurance that is instantly disproven. 'Not that low' is a perfect economy beat.

jokes.text: I've never been more proud I taught that man to ejaculate into a cup.
jokes.setup: The TV shows Richard heroically responding to a plane crashing into a 7-Eleven; Gary watches proudly
jokes.punchline: Gary's connection to Richard — and specifically how — is that he coached him through sperm donation. This replaces 'I taught him everything he knows' with a grotesquely specific act of mentorship
jokes.explanation: An extraordinary line. The structure subverts the mentor-pride cliché in the most specific, grotesque way possible. Gary's misplaced pride in this very particular form of coaching is both character-true (his need to be needed) and explosively funny. The specificity of 'into a cup' is essential — a less precise version would be far weaker. One of the best lines in the episode.

jokes.text: I've heard you had a yen for Treasury. / I think a lot of my supporters would actually think that State was more appropriate. / Ouch. Things are that bad at home, huh? / Let's just say a few international tours would ease domestic tensions.
jokes.setup: Selina is negotiating cabinet positions with Tom James in exchange for his endorsement
jokes.punchline: Tom's preference for State Department over Treasury — and Selina's immediate read that his marriage is in trouble — is confirmed by Tom's diplomatic admission that 'a few international tours would ease domestic tensions'
jokes.explanation: A nicely layered exchange where diplomatic language ('ease domestic tensions') is simultaneously sincere foreign policy speak and a personal confession about a troubled marriage. Selina's 'Ouch' read is sharp and efficient.

jokes.text: You familiar with 'peace through strength'? / Oh. Keep talkin' like Reagan, I'll work it like Nancy.
jokes.setup: Tom pitches his foreign policy philosophy
jokes.punchline: Selina's comeback implies she'll take a sexual/manipulative role analogous to Nancy Reagan if he keeps talking like Reagan — simultaneously flirtatious, political, and faintly grotesque
jokes.explanation: 'Keep talkin' like Reagan, I'll work it like Nancy' is a very quotable line — it's simultaneously a political joke about the Reagans and a sexual innuendo. The economy is excellent; the Nancy Reagan blowjob implication adds a layer of historical transgression that's very Veep.

jokes.text: Whoops. I guess we got ourselves a 'Gift of the Magi' situation here. / I was gonna ask you for the same thing.
jokes.setup: Two rival candidates are secretly meeting to negotiate an endorsement deal
jokes.punchline: Both candidates were coming to ask the other for a cabinet position in exchange for endorsement — they've both dropped out mentally while pretending to negotiate
jokes.explanation: The 'Gift of the Magi' reference is apt and well-deployed. The absurdity of two people negotiating from positions of mutual weakness is the political joke.

jokes.text: Were you gonna ask for Interior and then settle for HUD? / Dang it. So busted.
jokes.setup: The Gift of the Magi negotiation continues; Buddy said he wanted Interior
jokes.punchline: Jonah correctly guesses Buddy was going to bait-and-switch to HUD; Buddy's 'Dang it. So busted' confirms it like a kid caught cheating
jokes.explanation: 'Dang it. So busted.' from a gubernatorial candidate is a nicely juvenile button. Buddy's childlike delivery punctuates the scene well.

jokes.text: There is no way I would appoint anyone competent to anything in my cabinet.
jokes.setup: Someone asks Selina if she promised Tom James Secretary of State
jokes.punchline: Selina's reassurance is a confession of her governing philosophy — she doesn't appoint competent people, so naturally she didn't offer State
jokes.explanation: A masterfully compressed Selina character joke. It functions as reassurance while simultaneously being a damning confession about how she governs. Could only be Selina. High quotability — a single line that defines a character.

jokes.text: I'd like to give my strongest and most full-throated support to the person who has proven time and time again to be a born leader... but unfortunately that candidate has not yet emerged and shows no sign of doing so.
jokes.setup: Tom James is at a press conference; Selina and her team expect him to endorse her after their meeting
jokes.punchline: The buildup sounds exactly like an endorsement of Selina; the deflation is complete — 'that candidate has not yet emerged and shows no sign of doing so'
jokes.explanation: A devastating scene-level punchline. The structure of the non-endorsement — building maximally before collapsing — is perfectly constructed, and Selina's reaction ('He just fucked me right in the ass!') is a great button. The misdirection is engineered over multiple lines, making the payoff hit harder.

jokes.text: He just fucked me right in the ass! / Son of a bitch wouldn't endorse you. / That, too!
jokes.setup: Tom James just non-endorsed Selina on live TV
jokes.punchline: Selina's visceral reaction; Ben clarifies what happened; Selina clarifies that BOTH the non-endorsement AND the ass-fucking are problems
jokes.explanation: 'That, too!' is the punchline — Selina confirms she meant the ass-fucking is also a separate grievance, not just a metaphor for the endorsement. The beat depends on Ben's literal interpretation. Efficiently funny.

jokes.text: I mean, that's like blowing a rape whistle while you're raping somebody. / Exactly. / Speak truth to powerless.
jokes.setup: Selina wants to make a pro-police speech in a Black church to signal to white voters
jokes.punchline: Amy's rape whistle simile captures the perfect contradiction; Selina agrees enthusiastically; 'Speak truth to powerless' is a twisted rewriting of 'speak truth to power'
jokes.explanation: Two excellent jokes combined. The rape whistle simile is genuinely sharp — specific enough to be visceral, structural enough to illuminate the political contradiction exactly. 'Speak truth to powerless' is a brilliant inversion of an activist slogan that perfectly encapsulates Selina's cynical approach to minority outreach. Both Veep at its best.

jokes.text: Honeydew? If I wanna pretend to be in the CNN green room, I'll draw a face on Ben's ass and call that Christiane Amanpour.
jokes.setup: Someone brings Selina honeydew as fruit
jokes.punchline: Selina's rejection of honeydew becomes an absurdist insult riff: drawing Christiane Amanpour on Ben's ass would be a better substitute for CNN than serving honeydew is for real fruit
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully weird Selina rant. The logic — honeydew is to real fruit as Ben's ass is to Christiane Amanpour — is structurally absurdist but internally consistent. The specificity of 'Christiane Amanpour' elevates it far beyond a generic insult. Very high quotability.

jokes.text: Who said anything about winning? / You. I thought you said I was gonna be president. / No, you did. / Made me chuckle every time.
jokes.setup: Tanz is convincing Jonah not to drop out, despite terrible poll numbers
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Tanz never actually believed Jonah could win — 'Made me chuckle every time' — reframes Jonah's entire campaign as a cynical instrument
jokes.explanation: A strong structural reveal. 'Made me chuckle every time' is perfectly calibrated — not cruel enough to be a villain line, but dismissive enough to be devastating. The scene efficiently exposes the entire premise of Jonah's candidacy as a delusion.

jokes.text: Stricter marijuana laws! Ta-da! / I need potheads in my prisons. / Otherwise it's just murderers and rapists. / It makes for a very unpleasant atmosphere.
jokes.setup: Tanz reveals his actual agenda for keeping Jonah in the race: influencing the party platform
jokes.punchline: His policy goal is stricter marijuana laws — not for moral reasons, but because he needs marijuana users to balance out the murderers and rapists in his private prisons, for ambiance
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed dark comedy bit. The escalation from 'stricter marijuana laws' (seemingly a normal policy) to private prison ambiance optimization is a perfect satirical move. 'It makes for a very unpleasant atmosphere' is the line — delivered as a reasonable concern about prison decor. One of the episode's top five jokes.

jokes.text: I wish I'd aborted myself.
jokes.setup: Jonah is absorbing the revelation that his entire presidential campaign was a scheme
jokes.punchline: His response to the despair is a logistically impossible wish: self-abortion
jokes.explanation: Four words. Logistically impossible, darkly funny, perfectly Jonah. The joke works because it's both absurd (self-abortion is not a thing) and emotionally resonant (Jonah wishes he'd never existed). Economy is a 10. High quotability.

jokes.text: Plus, I need more casino licenses for Macau. / I know it sounds a little specific, / but in the end it'll benefit all Americans.
jokes.setup: Tanz has explained the marijuana law angle; now he reveals a second, even more specific hidden agenda
jokes.punchline: Macau casino licenses is so nakedly self-interested that Tanz has to acknowledge 'it sounds a little specific' — then insists it benefits all Americans, which is transparently false
jokes.explanation: Tanz's self-awareness about his corruption ('I know it sounds a little specific') makes it funnier than if he were purely deluded. The boilerplate 'it'll benefit all Americans' is a perfect political cliché grafted onto naked self-interest.

jokes.text: Permission to roll your breasts, ma'am? / Oh, granted.
jokes.setup: Selina is preparing to enter a church service; Marjorie is helping her dress/adjust
jokes.punchline: Marjorie asks formal permission for a deeply intimate physical act — treating breast-rolling as a military-style operational task requiring authorization
jokes.explanation: One of the best Marjorie lines in the series. The military/procedural register applied to breast management is absurdist gold — 'Permission to roll your breasts, ma'am?' has the cadence and formality of a battlefield request. Selina's 'Oh, granted' treats it as equally procedural. The joke is entirely in the register clash. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Because this is the Lord's house. Not the White House. / But soon to be the 'Black House.'
jokes.setup: Jordan is introducing Selina to the congregation and joking about church/state separation
jokes.punchline: 'Black House' — simultaneously a racial pun on White House and a statement about electing a Black president
jokes.explanation: A clean, crowd-pleasing pun. Works as church comedy; the wordplay is simple but effective. Not especially original but well-executed in context.

jokes.text: But the real victims are the police... ing. / Policing... that America does in the South China Sea.
jokes.setup: Selina is delivering what the congregation thinks will be a sympathetic speech about a child shot by police
jokes.punchline: She appears to say 'the real victims are the police' — a horrifyingly tone-deaf statement — then adds '...ing' to pivot to 'policing' and then pivots entirely to the South China Sea, completing her geopolitical detour
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best comedic construction. The structure is a double misdirection: first the audience (and congregation) hears 'the real victims are the police' — catastrophically wrong — then the '...ing' save appears to redirect to 'policing,' which could be okay, but then Selina pivots to the South China Sea entirely, completing the geopolitical money-grab that's been seeded all episode. It pays off multiple threads simultaneously. The trailing '...ing' is a structural masterstroke. Peak Veep writing.

jokes.text: It is time for America to recognize Chinese sovereignty over the Diaoyu Islands and the disputed mineral rights of the surrounding seabed. / Can I get an amen? / Woman: What? / Amen.
jokes.setup: Selina is now mid-pivot to Chinese geopolitics in a Black church
jokes.punchline: She asks for an 'amen' on sovereignty over the Diaoyu Islands; a confused congregant responds 'What?'; Selina ignores this and delivers her own 'Amen'
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the episode's main plot thread, executed with perfect cringe escalation. 'Can I get an amen?' on Chinese sovereignty is absurd; the congregant's 'What?' is the straight-man response; Selina providing her own 'Amen' is the defiant button. The payoff for the entire episode's Chinese money thread.

jokes.text: Today I have a BuzzFeed exclusive. We are getting the first national interview with Lurlene, Iowa's dog mayor, who it turns out is not a dog, but a human.
jokes.setup: Mike is launching his BuzzFeed webcast 'McLintalk'
jokes.punchline: His 'exclusive' is clarifying that the mayor of a town with a dog-sounding name is in fact human — treated as a major journalistic scoop
jokes.explanation: A good Mike beat — his journalistic standards are so low that 'he's actually human' qualifies as a BuzzFeed exclusive. Richard's presence as the mayor adds another layer.

jokes.text: I'm just a mayor who now, temporarily, has a Slurpee machine in his office.
jokes.setup: Richard is being asked about his heroism in the plane crash
jokes.punchline: His modest deflection — 'I'm no hero' — is followed by the detail that he does now have a Slurpee machine in his office, delivered as if this is a neutral administrative fact
jokes.explanation: A perfect Richard Splett joke. His deflection of heroism is immediately undercut by the incidental perk he's noticed — a Slurpee machine now in his office. The 'temporarily' qualifier is exquisite: he's already considered its duration. Completely deadpan. The specificity and Richard's lack of self-awareness are perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: Oh, good question. / Thank you. It's actually Wendy's.
jokes.setup: Mike asks Richard a question and Richard compliments it
jokes.punchline: Mike clarifies the question was actually written by his wife Wendy — treating journalistic credit with the same sincerity as a school project attribution
jokes.explanation: A nice small character beat for Mike — his incompetence extends to not having written his own interview questions. Efficient.

jokes.text: I guess I just did what anybody would do if they were there. / Except the people who were there and didn't do anything.
jokes.setup: Richard humbly says he just did what anyone would do
jokes.punchline: Mike's correction points out that the actual people there didn't do anything — quietly eliminating the premise of Richard's humility
jokes.explanation: A tight logic joke. Mike accidentally makes a good point by being literal — the humble cliché is undermined by the fact that it isn't true. Good structure.

jokes.text: Wo zai zehli bangmang. (I'm here to help in Mandarin) / Uh, is that Mandarin? / Yes, it was.
jokes.setup: Keith approaches Selina to discuss the Chinese money
jokes.punchline: He opens in Mandarin to signal his Chinese connections; Selina's genuine uncertainty ('Is that Mandarin?') followed by his 'Yes, it was' is a small perfect beat — his performance falls flat on the one person it needed to impress
jokes.explanation: A small but efficient character beat. Keith's signaling goes completely unrecognized; his 'Yes, it was' is a deflated confirmation.

jokes.text: I would say that we're well past the point of plausible deniability at this point.
jokes.setup: Selina begins to deny knowing about the Chinese money
jokes.punchline: Quinn stating the obvious — deniability is over — with complete calm as if giving a weather report
jokes.explanation: Functional but not exceptional. The deadpan observation is competent Veep but doesn't have the originality of the episode's stronger moments.

jokes.text: Well, I can't think of any other reason. / Well, let's be honest. God had a lot to do with it, too. / Oh, sure! Yeah. It's a miracle. / Let's go spend twenty-five million dollars on some racist robo callers. / Praise the Lord. / Right?
jokes.setup: Gary credits the $25M fundraising miracle to his faith-based video; Selina and Ben know it's Chinese money
jokes.punchline: 'Let's go spend twenty-five million dollars on some racist robo callers. / Praise the Lord.' — the 'Praise the Lord' applied to spending Chinese election money on racist calls is a perfect dark button
jokes.explanation: A beautiful structural contrast: Gary's innocent religious attribution vs. Ben and Selina's cynical knowledge. 'Praise the Lord' as a response to announcing racist robo callers is the dark irony crystallized. The scene layers three separate running threads simultaneously.

jokes.text: You know who else thinks that I don't have the intelligence or the 'tentrament' to be president? My very own campaign staff.
jokes.setup: Jonah is addressing a rally
jokes.punchline: 'Tentrament' — a mangled version of 'temperament' — delivered with complete confidence while making a speech about his intelligence
jokes.explanation: The malapropism in the middle of a speech defending his intelligence is a perfectly self-undermining character joke. 'Tentrament' is a very specific mangle — not 'temperment' but 'tentrament' — suggesting he's heard the word once and reconstructed it incorrectly. High character integration.

jokes.text: That is my campaign chair, Amy Brookheimer. She recently had an abortion.
jokes.setup: Jonah is pointing out members of his own campaign staff to the crowd as enemies
jokes.punchline: His first piece of opposition research on Amy is disclosing her abortion to his anti-abortion rally crowd
jokes.explanation: The flat, matter-of-fact disclosure — 'She recently had an abortion' — is delivered without fanfare, which makes it more devastating. The fact that this is Jonah's opposition research on his own staffer, delivered to a crowd, is both horrifying and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: And that is my chief strategist, Teddy Sykes, and he is an overgrown midget who had to be chemically castrated.
jokes.setup: Jonah continues pointing out his own campaign staff to the crowd
jokes.punchline: Teddy's introduction is 'overgrown midget who had to be chemically castrated' — a deeply personal and specific humiliation
jokes.explanation: Strong cringe beat. The specific detail 'chemically castrated' is a callback to Teddy's earlier storyline in the series; for viewers who know the context, it's a rewarding payoff.

jokes.text: And that guy right there, that's Eric something. / Bill Ericsson. / That's Eric Bill Ericsson, and he thinks he's better than everybody else in this room.
jokes.setup: Jonah is continuing his public humiliation tour of his own staff
jokes.punchline: He can't remember Bill Ericsson's name at all; when corrected, he mangles the correction; his 'opposition research' on Bill is simply 'he thinks he's better than you'
jokes.explanation: Three-beat joke: not knowing the name, mangling the correction, then the grade-school accusation 'thinks he's better than everybody.' The escalation from personal medical disclosure (Teddy) to simple playground accusation (Eric) is itself a joke about Jonah's diminishing research.

jokes.text: Math was created by Muslims. / Yeah. And we teach this Islamic math to children. / Math teachers are terrorists.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just told the crowd his stepfather revealed math was invented by Muslims
jokes.punchline: Three-step escalation: Muslims invented math → we teach children Islamic math → math teachers are terrorists
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the episode's anti-math running gag. Each step in the logic chain is individually absurd but follows from the previous with horrible internal consistency: if math is Islamic, and we teach it, then teachers are terrorists. The crowd's apparent receptiveness is what makes it a great political satire beat. Earns the 8.6.

jokes.text: I may be a registered sex offender, but I cannot be a part of this. I'm gone.
jokes.setup: Teddy watches Jonah declare math teachers terrorists and decides to leave
jokes.punchline: His ethical limit isn't sexual offending — it's anti-math rhetoric. The casual self-identification as a sex offender as a baseline for his integrity is the punchline
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode and a contender for best in the series. The structure is perfect: his moral outrage at the anti-math speech is undercut by the casual, parenthetical admission that he's a registered sex offender — and the implication is that sexual offending does NOT cross his ethical line, but this does. The character integration is exceptional (we know Teddy's history). Economy is near-perfect. Almost every word is load-bearing.

jokes.text: Algebra? More like Al Jazeera.
jokes.setup: Jonah is on a roll against Islamic math
jokes.punchline: He makes a sound-alike pun connecting algebra (from Arabic 'al-jabr') to Al Jazeera as evidence of Islamic infiltration
jokes.explanation: A deceptively clever line. 'Algebra? More like Al Jazeera' works because it's stupid-smart — it's the kind of pun a paranoid moron would make, but it's also technically true (algebra IS from Arabic). The joke is in the delivery of genuine linguistic coincidence as conspiracy. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Under a Ryan presidency, I will ban this Sharia math from being taught to American children. / No more math! / God fuck America. No more math! No more math!
jokes.setup: Jonah's anti-math rally is reaching its crescendo
jokes.punchline: 'Sharia math' as a policy enemy, and 'God fuck America' as a patriotic cry — the inversion of 'God bless America' with 'God fuck' is a deliberately wrong culmination
jokes.explanation: 'Sharia math' and 'God fuck America' are both great coinages. The crowd chanting 'No more math' is the satirical peak of the episode's Jonah subplot — a crowd united in opposition to mathematics is both absurd and pointed.

jokes.text: Well, I'll tell you one thing. If we lose, it certainly won't be for lack of touching people in a Denny's.
jokes.setup: The team is watching election returns nervously
jokes.punchline: Dan's dry observation that whatever happens, they physically touched voters in a Denny's — an achievement so meager it's absurd as consolation
jokes.explanation: A nice dry Dan moment. 'Touching people in a Denny's' is a vivid, specific image of desperate campaign retail politics. The understatement is well-calibrated.

jokes.text: Ben: Uh, like my prostate. / Mostly black and much larger than we'd like.
jokes.setup: Selina asks how turnout is looking
jokes.punchline: Ben compares it to his prostate — and the elaboration 'mostly black and much larger than we'd like' works on both a medical level (enlarged prostate, Black voters turning out) and a political level simultaneously
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The prostate comparison works on two levels simultaneously — medically accurate (enlarged prostates are common in older men, can be enlarged/inflamed) and politically apt (African American voter turnout is high). The double meaning is perfectly constructed and the specificity of 'mostly black' lands both readings at once. Ben's deadpan delivery and the word economy make this exceptional.

jokes.text: Madam President, would you like to say something to our viewers? / Sure, um, if you haven't voted, please-- / Oh, sorry. I'm getting a call. I apologize, it's Wendy.
jokes.setup: Mike is doing live election coverage; Selina is about to address his viewers
jokes.punchline: Mike interrupts a sitting presidential candidate mid-sentence to take a call from his wife — the domestic utterly overrides the political
jokes.explanation: A perfect Mike incompetence beat — interrupting the candidate he's supposed to be boosting for a personal call. Selina's reaction is implicit but devastating.

jokes.text: Ben, I sold my soul to the Chinese for a lousy twenty-five million dollars.
jokes.setup: Selina is processing the implications of the Chinese money
jokes.punchline: Her complaint isn't that she sold her soul — it's that she sold it cheap. 'Lousy twenty-five million' implies she knows souls should command a higher price
jokes.explanation: A sharp character revelation. The 'lousy' is the punchline — she's not upset about selling her soul, just the price. This is Selina's moral worldview in a single line.

jokes.text: You cannot trust the Chinese. / I married enough of them to know that. / Wait, isn't your wife Korean? / Maybe. Fog of war.
jokes.setup: Ben warns against the Chinese
jokes.punchline: 'Maybe. Fog of war.' — Ben doesn't know his own wife's ethnicity, and applies a military metaphor to his marriage
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly compressed character joke. 'Fog of war' applied to not knowing your wife's ethnicity is both a military-political satire gag and a character revelation about Ben's emotional disconnection from his personal life. The 'Maybe' before 'Fog of war' is the perfect hesitation. Very quotable.

jokes.text: It's a total blackout.
jokes.setup: Power outages are occurring in predominantly Black neighborhoods, suppressing the Black vote
jokes.punchline: 'Total blackout' simultaneously refers to the power outage and the suppression of Black voter participation — a double meaning that's uncomfortable and pointed
jokes.explanation: A dark double entendre that works as political satire. Dan almost certainly knows what he's saying; the script definitely does. Economy is perfect — two words carrying the full weight of voter suppression commentary.

jokes.text: From now on, I do what I want, I take what I want, whenever I want it. / 'New Selina Now.'
jokes.setup: Selina has just won South Carolina with Chinese help
jokes.punchline: 'New Selina Now' — branding her own unfettered corruption as a personal rebrand. The implication is that 'Old Selina' had limits; these are being eliminated
jokes.explanation: A strong character beat. 'New Selina Now' as a self-declared brand for unchecked corruption is pointed political satire. The self-awareness about her own shamelessness is the joke.

jokes.text: Oh, it's on the table by the chair. / So go get it.
jokes.setup: Selina wants to watch her victory speech again; no one knows where the remote is; someone locates it
jokes.punchline: Selina doesn't get the remote herself — she directs an aide to get it, illustrating 'New Selina Now' through the tiniest possible act of entitlement
jokes.explanation: A small beat that illustrates Selina's new entitlement mode. More of a character moment than a joke proper, but it earns a smile.

jokes.text: I'm surprised they let you run for president, because you look thirty-four, tops.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Gary if her eyes look puffy; he was previously dismissed in favor of Marjorie for this task
jokes.punchline: Gary's response to the puffiness question is pure sycophantic flattery — claiming she looks 34 when she clearly doesn't
jokes.explanation: A functional Gary sycophancy beat. Not especially original but character-true.

jokes.text: Like Jodie Foster in John Hinckley's diary. / Oh, my God. I'm obsessed with her.
jokes.setup: Selina is describing how Keith Quinn's name will be all over the faith-based outreach
jokes.punchline: 'Like Jodie Foster in John Hinckley's diary' — a perfect simile for unwanted obsessive attachment; Gary's 'I'm obsessed with her' response about Jodie Foster (not Hinckley) is the bonus beat
jokes.explanation: The Hinckley reference is a great simile for having your name involuntarily attached to something damaging. Gary's response about being obsessed with Jodie Foster — completely missing the dark implication — adds a perfect second beat.

jokes.text: So who's in charge of the faith-based outreach now? / Not it. / You are. [...] And Keith Quinn'll help you out too, I think. / Yes, there you go. So, your name will be all over it. Like Jodie Foster in John Hinckley's diary.
jokes.setup: Gary is being assigned the faith-based outreach despite it being a disaster; Selina reassures him his name will be 'all over it'
jokes.punchline: Ben's analogy: having your name 'all over' something like Jodie Foster's name was all over John Hinckley's diary — i.e., prominently associated with a catastrophe
jokes.explanation: A great dark historical reference. 'Like Jodie Foster in John Hinckley's diary' is a very specific, very dark analogy for unwanted notoriety — and Gary's response 'Oh, my God. I'm obsessed with her' completely misses the threat and takes it as a compliment. Gary's misread is the second beat of the joke.

jokes.text: Shouldn't you be pre-chewing my mother's food for her? / I told her her eyes looked puffy. / Thank you, chipmunk. / You're welcome, Catherine.
jokes.setup: Gary is in Selina's inner circle; Catherine makes a dismissive comment
jokes.punchline: Gary reveals he told Catherine her eyes looked puffy — unlike his sycophantic response to Selina — and Selina's approving 'Thank you, chipmunk' confirms she approves of Gary insulting Catherine
jokes.explanation: A callback that reveals Gary told Catherine the truth he withheld from Selina — inverting his usual dynamic. Selina rewarding him for insulting her daughter is the character beat. 'Thank you, chipmunk' is an oddly affectionate address.

jokes.text: Wait, I'm fired? Per fucking who?! / Per Selina. I believe her words were, 'If I need another Washington douche, I'll go to the M Street Rite Aid.'
jokes.setup: Dan discovers he's been fired from the campaign.
jokes.punchline: Selina's firing quote — 'If I need another Washington douche, I'll go to the M Street Rite Aid' — a geographically specific insult that equates Dan with a drugstore feminine hygiene product.
jokes.explanation: An excellent Selina insult — the specificity of 'M Street Rite Aid' (a real DC location) grounds the absurdity in a specific geography, making it feel authentically Selina-ish. 'Washington douche' as the noun for Dan is perfectly chosen — it's both a professional descriptor and a personal insult.

jokes.text: Wait, I'm fired? Per fucking who?! / Per Selina. I believe her words were, 'If I need another Washington douche, I'll go to the M Street Rite Aid.'
jokes.setup: Dan discovers he's been fired offscreen
jokes.punchline: Selina's exit line for firing Dan — 'If I need another Washington douche, I'll go to the M Street Rite Aid' — is a specific, vivid insult: calling him generically replaceable at a specific DC drug store
jokes.explanation: An excellent Selina insult. 'M Street Rite Aid' grounds it geographically in DC; the douche/Rite Aid pun is both literal (hygienic product at a drug store) and metaphorical. The specificity makes it land as more than a generic put-down — she's saying Dan is standard-issue Capitol Hill, available in bulk.

jokes.text: You should flip through your NDA. If you breathe a word of anything, to anyone, we'll slit your throat. / Later, gator!
jokes.setup: Dan is being fired; Leon threatens him with NDA consequences
jokes.punchline: Gary's 'Later, gator!' after Leon's death threat is the perfect tonal whiplash — cheerful dismissal immediately following a murder threat
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of Leon's cold threat ('we'll slit your throat') with Gary's breezy 'Later, gator!' is a perfect Veep tonal joke. Gary's obliviousness to the violence around him — or his complete comfort with it — makes him funnier and more disturbing simultaneously.

jokes.text: Third place was a massive win for us. Selina Meyer and Kemi Talbot can feel Jonah Ryan breathing down their necks.
jokes.setup: A reporter points out Jonah placed a distant third
jokes.punchline: Amy's spin — third is 'massive win' — is standard political BS, but framing the first and second place candidates as feeling Jonah's breath is absurd given his distant position
jokes.explanation: A functional political spin joke — Amy's ability to frame any result as a win is the character gag.

jokes.text: Jonah Ryan said he's been lying to everyone the whole time. Why should anyone trust him now? / Frankly, Jonah Ryan is the only candidate who's honest about his dishonesty.
jokes.setup: Reporter challenges Amy on Jonah's admitted dishonesty
jokes.punchline: Amy turns the admission of lying into a virtue: 'honest about his dishonesty' — a meta-honest claim that is itself a spin
jokes.explanation: A very sharp political spin joke. 'Honest about his dishonesty' is a genuine rhetorical inversion — Amy is spinning Jonah's confession of lying into a transparency argument. The logic is absurd but coherent, and Amy's conviction makes it land. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Tell my attorney to meet me in the next filler state we're in, 'cause last I heard, you can't run for office and be in prison. / Well, maybe in the House you can't.
jokes.setup: Selina mentions her legal troubles
jokes.punchline: Ben's correction — maybe in the House you can't run from prison — implies the Senate or executive offices have lower standards, a dark institutional joke
jokes.explanation: A solid institutional cynicism joke. The implication that incarcerated candidates are common in the Senate/executive is a good dark political gag.

jokes.text: Congressman Ryan, it's an honor. / Oh, yeah! This is like looking in a mirror! / Yeah, a hot mirror.
jokes.setup: Jonah is introduced to his new Secret Service detail — a very tall, odd-looking agent
jokes.punchline: Jonah says 'like looking in a mirror' (implying similar appearance); the agent accepts the comparison but upgrades it: 'a hot mirror' — simultaneously agreeing and insulting Jonah's looks
jokes.explanation: A clean two-beat joke. The agent's 'hot mirror' correction is a neat self-compliment that's simultaneously a subtle Jonah put-down. Economy is strong.

jokes.text: I have always been transparent about how old I am. / Yeah, 55. / I believe it's 53. / Late 40s. / You're all correct.
jokes.setup: Jonah claims transparency about his age
jokes.punchline: Three different people give three different ages, each lower; Jonah accepts all of them as 'correct' — revealing he either doesn't know his own age or is lying about it
jokes.explanation: A tight escalation joke about political vanity. The descending ages and Jonah's acceptance of all of them is structurally clean. The punchline 'You're all correct' is a perfectly Jonah response to an unanswerable contradiction.

jokes.text: I don't like when people get closer to talk to me. It usually means they're gonna be facing a longer prison sentence.
jokes.setup: The legal situation is worsening; someone approaches Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina has learned from experience that when someone gets physically close to deliver news, it means they're about to implicate themselves in something criminal — a dark empirical observation about her life
jokes.explanation: A great self-aware Selina line. The observation — people whisper bad news, and in her world bad news = prison — is both a character joke and a dark self-assessment. 'Longer prison sentence' implies she knows prison is already on the table.

jokes.text: ...I saw Andrew. / The prosecution rests.
jokes.setup: Someone mentions they saw Andrew (Selina's ex-husband, a known grifter)
jokes.punchline: Ben's 'The prosecution rests' — treating the mere mention of Andrew as conclusive evidence of wrongdoing
jokes.explanation: Three words that convey everything. Ben's legal-speak dismissal — Andrew's mere presence is proof enough — is perfect economy. Strong for those with series context.

tmdb_overview: Selina and team head to South Carolina, where she seeks an endorsement from a key leader. Gary gets a long-awaited promotion, while Marjorie excels in her new role.
insight: 64 jokes in 39 minutes—Veep's densest finale stretch sustains relentless wordplay.
